title: VLOOKUP
summary: VLOOKUP searches for a value in the first column of a table and returns a value from a column to its right. It is one of the most widely used lookup functions in business.
jobUseCases: Pulling an employee's salary from an HR table using their employee ID
Fetching a product's unit price into an order form from a price list
Looking up a customer's city based on their account number
Matching a course code to its title in a training records sheet
Bringing vendor details into a purchase order from a supplier master

examples.title: Exact match lookup
examples.explanation: Finds 'E102' in the first column (A) and returns the value from the 3rd column (C). FALSE forces an exact match, which is almost always what you want.

examples.title: Lookup from an input cell
examples.explanation: Looks up whatever ID is in G2 and returns the 4th column. Using a cell reference makes the lookup reusable for any value typed in.

examples.title: Approximate match for grades
examples.explanation: TRUE finds the closest value not exceeding B2 in a sorted bracket table, useful for grade or tax bands. The table's first column must be sorted ascending.

examples.title: Locking the table with absolute references
examples.explanation: Dollar signs freeze the table range so it does not shift when the formula is copied down the column. The lookup_value stays relative.

commonMistakes: Omitting FALSE so VLOOKUP defaults to approximate match and returns wrong results on unsorted data
Counting col_index_num from the wrong column; it counts from the first column of table_array, not the sheet
Trying to look left; VLOOKUP can only return columns to the right of the lookup column
Forgetting absolute references on table_array, so the range drifts when copied
Breaking formulas by inserting a column inside table_array, which shifts the col_index_num
practiceTips: Always use FALSE for exact matches unless you specifically need bracket lookups
Lock the table_array with $ signs before copying the formula down
Wrap in IFERROR to show a friendly message when a value is not found, or switch to XLOOKUP
